title: Understand: half-life and radiometric dating
description: Check your understanding of half-life and radiometric dating in this set of free practice questions.

assessment_items.question_content:
**Complete the statement.**

* Half-life is the time it takes for  $\_\_\_\_\_\_$ of a specific isotope to decay.

[[☃ radio 1]]

choices.content: a single nucleus
half of a sample
an entire sample

assessment_items.question_content:
**What is the definition of half-life?**

[[☃ radio 1]]

choices.content: the time it takes for $50$% of a radioisotope sample to decay
the time it takes for a single nucleus in a radioisotope sample to decay
half the time required for $100$% of a radioisotope sample to decay

assessment_items.question_content:
A sample of a certain radioisotope has a half-life of $1$ hour.

**Based on this information, which of the following statements is true?**

[[☃ radio 1]]

choices.content: After one hour has passed, half the sample remains.
In two hours, the sample is fully decayed.
The radioisotopes won’t begin decaying until one hour has passed.

assessment_items.question_content:
A scientist starts with $6.0$ grams of a pure carbon-$14$.

**What additional information is needed to determine the half-life of this radioisotope?**


[[☃ radio 1]]

choices.content: the time it takes for $3.0$ grams of the sample to decay
whether carbon-$14$ decays by alpha or beta emission
the fact that the sample contains $0.43$ moles of carbon-$14$

assessment_items.question_content:
A researcher is studying one mole of a specific radioisotope.

**What information is needed to determine the half-life of the sample?**

[[☃ radio 1]]

choices.content: how long it takes until only half a mole remains
whether the sample decays by alpha or beta emission
the atomic mass of the sample

assessment_items.question_content:
The element Meitnerium was first synthesized in 1982, and was named after Lise Meitner, one of the pioneers of nuclear science. 

[[☃ image 1]]

A lab has produced a sample of Mt-$278$. At a certain time, there are $50{,}000$ nuclei in the sample. 

**What additional information is needed to determine the half-life of this radioactive isotope?**

[[☃ radio 1]]

[[☃ explanation 1]]

options.explanation: “[Lise Meitner around 1906 in Vienna](https://commons.wikimedia.org/wiki/File:Lise_Meitner12.jpg)” by unknown photographer, [CC BY-SA 4.0](https://creativecommons.org/licenses/by-sa/4.0/)

options.alt: A photograph of nuclear physicist Lise Meitner, taken in Vienna in 1906.
options.caption: *Lise Meitner in 1906*

choices.content: the time required for $25{,}000$ nuclei to decay
how many nuclei were present in the original sample
the mass of the sample in grams

assessment_items.question_content:
Because meteorites were not formed on earth, the age of a meteorite offers clues about when our solar system was formed. The oldest meteorites are over $4.5$ billion years old!

[[☃ image 1]]

One method used to determine the age of meteorites uses the radioisotope rubidium-$87$.

**What property about $\ce{Rb-87}$ makes it useful for radiometric dating?**

[[☃ radio 1]]

[[☃ explanation 2]]

options.alt: A 1.7 kg meteorite from the Sikhote Alin meteorite shower of 12 February 1947. Found in eastern Siberia.
options.caption: *A 12-cm meteorite discovered in Siberia*

choices.content: $\ce{Rb-87}$ has a half-life of $49$ billion years.
$\ce{Rb-87}$ decays by emitting beta particles.
$\ce{Rb-87}$ easily forms ions with a $2+$ charge.

assessment_items.question_content:
*Australopithecus afarensis* is an extinct species of hominid and is part of the genus that gave rise to human beings. The most famous *Australopithecus* fossil is “Lucy,” discovered in Ethiopia in 1974. She’s believed to be $3.2$ million years old!

[[☃ image 1]]

Initial attempts to find Lucy’s age used samples of potassium in the surrounding rocks.

**What property about $\ce{K-40}$ makes it useful for radiometric dating?**

[[☃ radio 1]]

[[☃ explanation 1]]

options.alt: A reconstruction of the *Australopithecus afarensis* skeleton known as "Lucy," cast from the Museum national d'histoire naturelle in Paris.
options.caption: *A cast of the famous “Lucy” skeleton*

choices.content: $\ce{K-40}$ has a half-life of $1.25$ billion years.
$\ce{K-40}$ is an alkali metal.
When $\ce{K-40}$ undergoes beta minus decay, the daughter has the same mass number.

assessment_items.question_content:
In 2008, a team of scientists claimed the Nuvvuagittuq Greenstone Belt in Canada contained rocks dating back $4.3$ billion years! This would make these rock formations the oldest rocks discovered so far on Earth.

[[☃ image 1]]

The scientists determined the age of the rocks using the radioisotope samarium-$147$. 

**What property about $\ce{Sm-147}$ makes it useful for radiometric dating?**

[[☃ radio 1]]

[[☃ explanation 1]]

options.alt: The Nuvvuagittuq Greenstone Belt in Canada.
options.caption: *Nuvvuagittuq Greenstone Belt*

choices.content: $\ce{Sm-147}$ has a half-life of $107$ billion years.
$\ce{Sm-147}$ decays by emitting alpha particles.
$\ce{Sm-147}$ is the most common radioactive isotope of samarium.

assessment_items.question_content:
**Which statement is true about the half-life of a radioactive isotope?**

[[☃ radio 1]]

choices.content: The half-lives of different isotopes can vary from nanoseconds to billions of years.
After two half-lives have passed, a radioactive sample is considered fully decayed.
The half-life of an isotope can be changed using technology like X-rays or powerful magnetic fields.

assessment_items.question_content:
**Which statement is true about the half-life of a radioactive isotope?**

[[☃ radio 1]]

choices.content: Half-life can predict the decay of a sample of radioisotopes, but not each individual nucleus.
After two half-lives have passed, a radioactive sample is considered fully decayed.
All radioisotopes of the same element have the same half-life.

assessment_items.question_content:
**Which statement is true about the half-life of a radioactive isotope?**

[[☃ radio 1]]

choices.content: There is no known method of changing a radioisotope’s half-life.
Half-life can be used to predict when a single nucleus in a radioisotope sample will decay.
The half-lives of radioisotopes are typically around $10{,}000$ years.
